title: Median of Two Sorted Arrays
statement: You're given two sorted arrays `nums1` and `nums2` of sizes m and n. Return the median of the two arrays combined. The overall run time complexity must be O(log(m+n)).
constraints: Both arrays are sorted in ascending order

test_cases.explanation_if_failed: One array can be completely empty — the binary search must handle an empty partition range (i can be 0 or m).
nums1's values are all larger than nums2's — a solution that assumes interleaving values rather than doing true binary search on the partition can get this wrong.

brute_force.description: Merge both sorted arrays into one sorted array of length m+n, then index into the middle (averaging the two middle elements if the length is even).
brute_force.why_insufficient: This is correct and often fast enough in practice, but the problem explicitly requires O(log(m+n)) — merging is linear, not logarithmic, so it doesn't meet the stated requirement.

optimal_approach.description: Binary search on the smaller of the two arrays to find a partition index such that everything to the left of the combined partition is <= everything to the right. The partition index in the second array is determined by the first array's guess and the target half-size.

hint_ladder.text: The naive way is to merge and index into the middle — that's O(m+n). The problem wants O(log(m+n)); what technique usually gets you logarithmic time?
Binary search — but not over values. Try binary searching over a partition index instead.
You want to split the combined arrays into a left half and a right half of (nearly) equal size, where every element in the left half is <= every element in the right half.

Binary search on the smaller array's partition index; the partition index in the other array is `half_size - i`. If left1 > right2, your partition in the first array is too far right — search left. Otherwise search right.

common_loopholes.description: One of the two arrays can be entirely empty.
Must binary search the SMALLER array to guarantee the log bound; searching the larger one breaks the complexity requirement.
Even total length needs the average of two middle values; odd needs a single middle value.

key_points: Both input arrays are already individually sorted
Must run in O(log(m+n)) — a full merge does NOT satisfy this
Median definition differs for even vs odd combined length

complexity_probe.arg_generator:
import random
def _generate_complexity_args(n):
    random.seed(4680 + n)
    half = max(1, n // 2)
    nums1 = sorted(random.randint(-1_000_000, 1_000_000) for _ in range(half))
    nums2 = sorted(random.randint(-1_000_000, 1_000_000) for _ in range(max(1, n - half)))
    return {'nums1': nums1, 'nums2': nums2}
